original_abstract: Astrophysicists have long utilized chemical models to explore the processes governing the chemical composition of gas and dust in the interstellar medium (ISM). Recently, the predictive power of these models has improved significantly due to advancements in experimental techniques and theoretical methods. These developments allow researchers to study individual reaction rate constants under conditions that closely mimic the extreme environments of the ISM. Furthermore, because chemical compositions in star-forming regions are far from a steady state, contemporary models increasingly incorporate the dynamical evolution of physical conditions. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the state of the art in chemical modeling for dark clouds—the critical initial stage in the formation of stars and planetary disks. Combining insights from current kinetic databases and reaction networks, we assess how well these models reproduce observed molecular abundances and discuss the ongoing challenges in bridging the gap between theory and observation.
humanized_abstract: The chemistry of the interstellar gas and dust is currently studied through numerical models. Such models have been continuously improved in the recent years due to the progress in laboratory studies, which allow for a precise determination of the reaction rate constants under the ISM conditions, and theoretical modelling, which is no longer in equilibrium in star forming regions. In this paper we provide a review of the current status of the dark cloud chemical modelling. By comparing the latest kinetic databases and reaction models, we discuss the ability of the present day models to fit the observed molecular abundances, and analyse the main issues which need to be tackled in order to progress towards a fully consistent theory.
